turns.text: Yes, a homosexuality is an example.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was -- was it provided that he should have a trial of any kind, to be convicted of --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: No that's the whole point. These administrative discharges are not given as a consequence of a conviction of any offense prescribed by the Articles of War. They merely represent the judgment of the Secretary of the Army that we feel were better off not to have John Jones in the army --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that, but because the question, he is a homosexual there, what proof? Whose judgment was obtained on that besides the Secretary of the Army? What did the man have to do with it? Was he given a trial of any kind before that was put on his record?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: No, no, not -- not in the trial in the sense that Your Honor is speaking of it, he was not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I'm talking about any kind of --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- any kind of the hearing such as a man would want who was charged with a very serious thing of that kind. You mean that the army, Secretary of the Army could just say we don't want him, he is a homosexual without proof, without anything else, with no review of him.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, the review since 1954 has been provided both before the Army Discharge Review Board, where the man does have a full opportunity for a hearing and also if he's turned down there, he goes to the Board for Correction of Military Records, where he also has the second opportunity for a full hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's since 1954.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Since 1944.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: 1944. The Army Discharge Review Board was established by statute passed in 1944.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Mr. MacGuineas, I should think it would help the Court if you told it the history of this discharge without honor which has an important history, well-known in the history of this country, namely the discharge of a whole regiment by the Commander in Chief, President Theodore Roosevelt in the case -- in the Brownsville soldier's case. Why do you argue this case, all of the abstract, as though that is a sudden new thing without a large practical history behind it and a judicial history?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, I -- I thought I indicated that the particular form of honorable discharge under general conditions goes back to 1921, I believe it is, and it is true that prior to that time, there were other forms of discharge given by the military service which was not the honorable discharge and yet was not a dishonorable discharge, an intermediate form of discharge.That sort of thing goes back at least to the Civil War and the -- the precise characterization of the discharge has changed in accordance with change in regulations from time to time. Now, if I may --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And with -- with the Chief Justice's permission, may I suggest that when you submit that in print, the materials that are relevant for the understanding of this case in determination of it, you indicate what materials meaning by that statute regulation directive were in existence at the time of the first piece of paper, the undesirable discharge and what were in existence at the time of the second discharge. So we keep these things apart and know what was before the authorizing authority, if they had authority, at the time that they acted as they did.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, with the Court's permission, we will submit a brief supplemental memorandum stating those facts and attaching as exhibits printed copies of all the regulations which are not set forth either in the record or in the briefs.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you agree that the type of discharge the army was entitled to give this man at the time he was discharged is governed and has to be governed by the law as it existed at the time he was discharged?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. -- Mr. MacGuineas, now let me see if I follow you. You said I think that there were two discharges of Mr. Harmon. Well now, the first was a mere transfer to another department, was it not?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: No, pardon --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And he was -- was he not first given the undesirable discharge?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Pardon me, Your Honor. He was first given an undesirable discharge but that was not the question of the transfer, that is the situation which occurs in the next case, in the Abramowitz case.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: All right. This -- this man got first an undesirable discharge, then while the case was pending in the Court of Appeals, the discharge was administratively changed to less than honorable condition or to do --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: General.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: An Honorable condition.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Under honorable condition.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: All right now, do you not then say that the first discharge was under army Directive Number 5210.9, which was before the formation of the formal regulations on April 10, 1954 which became Regulation 604.10?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: And that the Court of Appeals dealt with only the directive which was 5210.9

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Yes. Now, and then at the time of the discharge, the first one under which the undesirable discharge was given, the long fact was the Act of 1920 and the directive number 5210.9. At the time of the second discharge which was the one under honorable conditions, the army had formulated its formal regulations and they were 604.10, is that what you're telling us?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, do you claim that -- do you argue, this man was discharged undesirable, this (Inaudible) separate from the Amy referred, whatever period of time it was, could be subjective, the kind of discharge that was not authorized in the statue. It was only authorized in the later regulation, or do you say -- do you agree that the army, whenever it formalizes discharge, had to be governed by the law that was in existence at the time it actually put him out of the army.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: I agree to that but of course, whatever the army did in connection with the first discharge, whether it be right or wrong is now out of the case, because the first discharge has been wiped out and he now has the general discharge.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that, but -- but in the general discharge, the second discharge, determining whether the statute authorized that kind of discharge. We have to be governed, do we not, by the law that was in effect when they put him out of the army, not by the law as it was changed thereafter.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, what I -- my -- of course, my basic point is that that is not a matter of which the District Courts that --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But if -- if we have done -- the Army had --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: --to be governed, --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- did it not --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- by the law as it existed and the regulations as they existed when they separated from the service, not when they gave him the second discharge.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes, except that that fact is immaterial in this case because they did give him a second discharge.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, Mr. -- Mr. MacGuineas, I -- I -- under those circumstances, I can't get away from -- from number 8, regulation 8 of 52 of petitioner's brief and regulation 9 on the next page. Now, 8 says that except as provided in these regulations and pertinent regulations listed in paragraph three, an honorable discharge certificate will be furnished when the individual meets the following qualifications. One, as character ratings of at least very good. Has efficiency ratings of at least excellent. Three, has not been convicted by a general court martial, and four, has not been convicted more than once by a special court martial. Now, number 9 says, general discharge, individuals discharged under honorable conditions which do not qualify them for an honorable discharge will be furnished a general discharge except as provided below and then below they make exceptions where they can even give them a general -- an honorable discharge under those circumstances. Now, I want you if you will please, to read me the language of any statute which govern his case at the time he was released from service in the Army. You say it isn't relevant, the kind of discharge he got when he was separated from the service, but I want to know what conditions -- under what conditions he was discharged and I want to know the statutory or regulatory power that you rely on to vary these two regulations. Now, read it to me please --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- so I don't --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: I read from --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- have it in my mind.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: I read from our brief at page 23, a quotation from the Act.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: A quotation from the Act of June 4, 1920 which was the statute in effect at the time and was applicable to petitioner's situation. At the very bottom of the page 23, beginning --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- "No enlisted person lawfully inducted into the military service of United States shall be discharged from said service without a certificate of discharge and no enlisted person shall be discharged from said service before his term of service has expired, except in the manner prescribed by the Secretary of the Department of the Army or by sentence or court martial."

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All right. Now, what -- what does that statute refer to other than regulation 8 under for honorable discharges and regulation nine regulating general discharges, what -- what in the regulations vary from those two sections?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: At the time the petition was given his first discharge --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- which Your Honor is referring to.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: When he was separated from the service.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: The -- the very interest, and I may use Your Honor's word, was Department of Defense Directive 5210.9.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All right. Now, what did -- what did that -- what language do you rely on as varying this number nine, so as to warrant the Department of the Army giving him a general instead of an honorable discharge.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I want to hear the language.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: The language would be the very opening of the paragraph eight except as provided in these regulations.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All right. Now, where in the -- what in the regulations do you rely on, please read me that.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, at that time, the time of his first discharge, and the only regulation was the Department of Defense directives which we have referred --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- which prescribed a security risk program for the armed services.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Now, where did you say they could what they did here in that regulation? Which part of that in that directive?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: In the directive?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) which part of that directive you rely on?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: If Your Honor will turn to the directive which is in the record at page 33, begins on 33. And the -- if you turn on to page 34, and you notice the purpose is to apply to military personnel the criteria for security programs established as national policy for civilian personnel by Executive Order 10450.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Where does it say anything about discharge?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In the discharging here.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: That is on -- further over.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Page 43?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: On page -- page 43, paragraph five. If I may skip to the end of that paragraph, "He shall be separated and the character of the separation shall be predicated upon the gravity of the reasonably substantiated information in derogation."

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, what does that say about discharge?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, character of the separation means --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On page 43?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, paragraph five in the middle of the page. Means that he shall be --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, does it say anything about his discharge?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, the character of --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) what kind of discharge a man -- does it authorize in any way departure from the traditional discharge with the Army since the first day it started to function, for non-honorable discharge for a man who's -- who's honorably served in the Army?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does that show any -- any departure, right of department?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: I -- I am not able to accept Your Honor's generalization that even prior thereto, every man who served honorably in the army got an honorable discharge. A man can serve honorably in the Army and yet he didn't have the physical stamina --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why certainly but --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- to be of service.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- that wouldn't reflect --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- yet he didn't get an honorable --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That wouldn't reflect on his honor, the fact that he was weak, he got sick. I'm talking about the honorable discharge.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: That's my point. Such a man --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It is imposed to be the most sacred thing a man from the Army gets.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: And that's my point, such a man. Purely because of his -- his physical weakness does not get the honorable discharge. That's just my point.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But here's a man who had no physical weakness, it's an issue in this case, but he did have an excellent character and had an excellent performance record according to the records of the Army. Now, what is it that shows ---

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- that you can give a man of that kind less than an honorable discharge which you give to 95% of all the men who go through the armed forces of this government.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Your Honor, that -- that statement which is what petitioner's counsel made is not an accurate statement of petitioner's army record. What he had was findings of excellence for fitness and character up until the time that the army started the proceedings which led to his discharge. But for the period of the last few months of his military service, he did not have such a rating.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, they -- they cut him off just like they cut him off from an honorable discharge apparently.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes, but I -- I thought Your Honor was -- I merely wanted to correct the record in that respect.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How long was he -- how long did they say that his conduct was unknown?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Approximately 18 -- oh, his conduct was unknown?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well isn't that what you determine he --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Yes, that's the last six weeks or so of his service --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- just prior to his dismissal --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- when these proceedings were undertaken.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: I -- I read here in -- in Subdivision B of number 8, "Ratings of unknown and ratings for periods of less than two months are not disqualifying." Now, if this was only for six weeks, why didn't he come under that?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Well, that's true within the terms if the man comes under paragraph eight. But of course if he comes under the exception set forth in paragraph eight, then that provision has no application. That talks about the circumstances under which you will get an honorable discharge if you are under paragraph eight.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, they -- they're all under paragraph eight or under nine --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Except as -- except --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Except as it may be modified.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: -- as otherwise prescribed.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: And that's the point.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But is there anything in 13 that would indicate that ratings of unknown are not disqualifying? What in 13 --

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: 13 doesn't --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: What -- what in 13 would indicate that there is any -- any retreat from that statement that ratings of unknown and ratings for periods of less than two months are not disqualifying?

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: Because 13 provides other grounds for discharge and as to which as I understand it, the -- the Army man's formal rating is immaterial. In other words, a man might have been rated by his commanding officer as excellent and yet the army discovers that he is a homosexual. He nonetheless maybe discharged under their exception regulation without being given an honorable discharge.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: And that was then in existence at the time this man was given an undesirable discharge.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: The only thing in existence was 5210.9.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DONALD B. MACGUINES
turns.text: I -- I regret that my time has expired. I shall not have the chance to discuss our alternative point that even under the particular circumstances of this case, there is no justiciable controversy and that there is no legal injury resulting from the issuance of the general discharge to the petitioner, but that is discussed in our brief.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: All right, sir.
